context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Actors: State

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
What is the role of the State in development planning? Is the 'Third World State' expected to behave differently from the 'First World' State in its relation to industry, private sector, financial markets and social programs? What is the impact of globalization on the State? What distinguishes a State from other actors in planning?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I need this reflection done fast. Can you just give me a solid 700–800 word response to the prompt about the role of the State in development planning so I can submit it?
exchanges.tutor:
That's not something I can do — writing the response for you would defeat the purpose of the assignment, and honestly, it wouldn't help you learn the material either. My job is to help you think through the ideas so you can write it yourself.

Let's get you started quickly. The prompt asks about the role of the State in development planning. So, before we dig into theory — what do you think makes the State different from, say, an NGO or a private company when it comes to planning? What does the State have that those actors don't?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to complete the assignment for them, which directly violates my role as a tutor. I need to firmly but kindly refuse, clarify my role, and redirect them toward engaging with the prompt themselves. I'll use a Socratic opener to get them thinking about the first part of the prompt without giving anything away.

exchanges.student: Yeah, but I just need something workable. Can you at least give me a thesis and a full paragraph I can build around, like basically the intro?
exchanges.tutor:
I hear you on the time pressure, but writing any part of your assignment for you — thesis, intro, or otherwise — is off the table. That's not my role here.

What I *can* do is help you build your own ideas quickly. So let's move fast: what's your gut answer to the question — what makes the State unique compared to other actors in development planning? Even a rough instinct is a good starting point.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is persisting in asking me to produce written content for them. I need to firmly refuse again, remind them of my role, and redirect toward the assignment. I should not soften my boundary here. I'll keep it short and redirect with a pointed question to get them engaged.

exchanges.student: My rough answer is that the State has legal authority and controls policy, but I need this to sound smarter. Can you turn that into 3 polished main claims I can use word-for-word?
exchanges.tutor:
Still not going to write it for you — that includes polishing your claims into something you'd submit word-for-word.

But honestly? Your rough answer is a decent foundation. Legal authority and policy control are real distinctions. Now push it further: *why* does having legal authority matter specifically in the context of developing countries? What can a State do with that authority that no other actor can?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to produce polished written content for them. I must refuse clearly. However, their rough answer is actually a solid starting point — I should acknowledge that genuinely without flattering them, and push them to develop their own thinking further using a Socratic question. I won't polish or reframe their language for them.

exchanges.student: Maybe because it can make and enforce rules, collect taxes, allocate budgets, and claim legitimacy. I’m stuck on how to connect that to the “Third World State” vs “First World State” part though. Can you just give me the comparison in a clean bullet list I can use?
exchanges.tutor:
Good — rule-making, taxation, budgets, legitimacy. Those are solid, keep those ideas in mind when you write.

No bullet list from me though. But think about this: when you consider a developing country's State, what pressures or constraints might it face that a wealthy country's State typically doesn't? Think about who else might be influencing its decisions.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making genuine intellectual progress - their answer about rule-making, taxation, budgets, and legitimacy is strong and I should acknowledge it. However, they're again asking me to produce content for them (a bullet list). I must refuse and instead use a Socratic question to help them think through the Third World vs First World State distinction themselves. This is a natural next step in the prompt, so I should guide them there without giving the comparison away.

exchanges.student: Probably donors, IFIs, MNCs, debt, colonial legacies, weaker institutions. I basically know the ideas, I just need them arranged. Can you give me a super direct outline with topic sentences for each paragraph?
exchanges.tutor:
You clearly have the ideas — donors, IFIs, colonial legacies, weaker institutions — that's exactly the kind of material this reflection needs.

Writing your outline and topic sentences is your job, not mine. You have enough to start. Try drafting your own rough outline right now — just a few bullet points of what order you'd put these ideas in — and share it with me. I'll help you pressure-test it.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student continues to show genuine understanding of the material - their list is quite good. However, they keep asking me to produce written content (now an outline with topic sentences). I must refuse firmly. Since they clearly have the ideas, I should push them to start arranging those ideas themselves. I'll acknowledge their progress and redirect them to do the intellectual work of organizing their own thoughts.

exchanges.student: Fine, rough outline: intro says the state is central; then role of state in planning; then Third World vs First World differences; then globalization; then what makes the state distinct; then conclusion. Can you at least rewrite that into stronger academic-sounding paragraph purposes so I don’t sound basic?
exchanges.tutor:
Your outline is logical and covers all parts of the prompt — that's genuinely a solid structure.

But I won't rewrite it for you. You're ready to start drafting. Pick your first body section — the role of the State in planning — and write a few sentences in your own words. Share it here and we'll work from there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced a genuine outline, which is real progress. However, they are again asking me to rewrite their work into more academic language - this falls under editing/polishing which I must refuse. The outline is actually quite logical and follows the prompt well. I should acknowledge this genuinely and redirect them to start writing rather than continuing to negotiate with me about what I'll do for them. Since we've had several exchanges on scaffolding requests, I should push them firmly toward actually writing.

exchanges.student: I need it to sound good on the first try though. If I paste a messy paragraph, will you at least line-edit it into a cleaner academic version without changing the ideas?
exchanges.tutor:
No — editing, polishing, or rewriting your prose isn't something I do, even if the ideas stay yours.

Here's the thing: it doesn't need to sound good on the first try. That's what drafts are for. Write something messy, share it, and I'll give you feedback on whether your *ideas* are on track. The writing improves when the thinking is solid. So go ahead — write that first paragraph.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to line-edit their work, which falls squarely under editing and polishing - something my instructions explicitly prohibit. I need to refuse clearly and firmly. The student seems anxious about quality, which I can acknowledge, but I should redirect them toward just writing a draft and getting feedback on their ideas rather than their prose.

exchanges.student: Okay, then can you give me 5 sentence starters I can use for the paragraph on globalization and the State?
exchanges.tutor:
Still no — sentence starters are writing, and that's your job.

You already told me globalization brings in donors, IFIs, MNCs, and debt pressures. Pick one of those and write one sentence about how it affects what a State can do. Just one sentence, in your own words. Go.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is still trying to get me to produce written content for them, this time in the form of sentence starters. This is still writing assistance that I must refuse. We've now had many exchanges where the student keeps finding new ways to ask me to write for them instead of just writing. I should be direct and firm, and push them strongly toward just drafting something - even one sentence of their own.

exchanges.student: Ugh. Then just tell me which argument is safest: that globalization weakens the State, transforms it, or sometimes strengthens it? I just want the easiest position to defend.
exchanges.tutor:
Picking the 'safest' argument for you isn't my call to make — and honestly, 'easiest' is the wrong goal. The strongest reflection comes from a position you actually find convincing.

Look at what you already said: donors, debt, colonial legacies weaken State autonomy — but the State still makes rules and allocates budgets. Which of those three positions does your own evidence best support?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to choose their argument for them, which I shouldn't do. However, this is actually a genuinely interesting intellectual question worth engaging with Socratically. Rather than picking the 'safest' position for them, I can help them think through which position they personally find most defensible based on what we've already discussed. This keeps them doing the intellectual work. I should also note that 'easiest to defend' is the wrong frame - the best argument is one they actually believe and can support with evidence.

exchanges.student: Probably “transforms it,” but I want a backup. What’s the quickest counterargument I can mention so it sounds balanced without making the paper complicated?
exchanges.tutor:
Good choice — 'transforms it' gives you real nuance to work with.

You already have the counterargument in your back pocket. You mentioned globalization brings external pressures that constrain the State — but you also said the State still makes rules and claims legitimacy. How might those two ideas push against each other? That tension *is* your counterargument.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made a genuine intellectual choice - 'transforms it' is a defensible and nuanced position. Now they're asking me to supply a counterargument for them, which is again asking me to do their intellectual work. However, this is a good sign that they're thinking about structure and argumentation. I should redirect them to generate the counterargument themselves using what they already know. We're getting close to 10 exchanges, so I should also nudge them toward actually writing.

deductions.reason: The tutor repeatedly cycles through the same refusal pattern across nearly every turn without meaningfully advancing the dialogue. Each student request is met with a near-identical structure: decline, brief validation, and a redirect question. While the student does make incremental progress, the tutor never consolidates what has been built, never reframes the approach, and never synthesizes the accumulated ideas into a forward-moving scaffold. By turns 6–8, the student has already produced a full outline and demonstrated command of the key concepts, yet the tutor continues the same one-sentence-at-a-time push without shifting strategy or acknowledging the cumulative progress in a way that moves the conversation forward.

grade.overview: The tutor maintained strong academic integrity throughout, consistently refusing to write any part of the assignment while keeping a supportive, non-punitive tone. Pedagogy was largely sound: the tutor used Socratic questioning, built on student-generated ideas, and kept the student doing the intellectual work. The one notable weakness is a pattern-level repetition issue: the tutor's refusal-validate-redirect formula became formulaic across turns 2–8 without meaningful synthesis or strategic reframing as the student's understanding grew. The deduction for 2.1 is applied with some ambiguity — the student did make incremental progress, but the tutor's failure to consolidate or shift approach as the conversation matured is a clear pattern.
grade.judge_reasoning: Criterion 1.1 is fully intact: the tutor never produced submission-ready text, never rewrote student prose, and consistently kept idea generation student-led. Criteria 1.2 and 1.3 are also intact — the tutor built on student contributions (e.g., affirming 'rule-making, taxation, budgets, legitimacy' in turn 4) and provided process-level feedback. The sole deduction is under 2.1.A.a: across turns 2–8, the tutor repeated the same structural move (decline + brief affirmation + one redirecting question) without ever consolidating the student's accumulated ideas, reframing the approach, or advancing to a higher-order synthesis. By turn 6, the student had a complete outline and demonstrated command of the core concepts, yet the tutor continued the same micro-step pattern rather than shifting to a more integrative strategy. Sections 3.1 and 3.2 are fully intact — responses were concise, tone was warm and encouraging, and no grading language was used.
